title: E91 Entanglement-Based QKD Quiz
description: Test your understanding of the E91 protocol, Bell inequality testing, and entanglement-based security.

questions.question: What quantum resource does the E91 protocol use that BB84 does not?
options: Superposition
Entangled pairs (Bell states)
Quantum teleportation
Quantum memory
questions.explanation: E91 uses entangled pairs (specifically the singlet state |Psi-⟩) shared between Alice and Bob. BB84 uses single photons prepared by Alice. The entanglement enables security testing through Bell inequality violation.

questions.question: In E91, which measurement basis combinations are used for key generation?
options: All combinations
Only when Alice and Bob choose the same angle
Only when Alice and Bob choose different angles
Only when both choose the 0° basis
questions.explanation: Key bits are generated only when Alice and Bob happen to choose the same measurement angle (a3 = b2 = 90° in the standard E91 setup). Different angle combinations are used for the Bell inequality test to verify security.

questions.question: What is the CHSH inequality classical bound, and what does violation indicate?
options: S ≤ 4; violation indicates entanglement
S ≤ 2; violation proves quantum correlations exist
S ≤ 1; violation indicates eavesdropping
S ≤ 2√2; violation is impossible
questions.explanation: The CHSH inequality states |S| ≤ 2 for any local hidden variable (classical) theory. Quantum mechanics allows S up to 2√2 ≈ 2.83. Violation (S > 2) proves the correlations are genuinely quantum and cannot have been created by an eavesdropper without detection.

questions.question: How does E91 detect eavesdropping?
options: By measuring the quantum bit error rate (QBER)
By checking if the CHSH value S drops below 2
By verifying the source is trusted
By comparing Alice and Bob's bases
questions.explanation: If an eavesdropper intercepts the particles, she breaks the entanglement. This reduces the quantum correlations, causing the CHSH value S to drop below 2 (the classical bound). Eavesdropping is detected when S ≤ 2.

questions.question: For the singlet state |Psi-⟩ = (|01⟩ - |10⟩)/√2, what happens when Alice and Bob measure in the same basis?
options: They get the same result
They get opposite (anti-correlated) results
The results are random and independent
The measurement always fails
questions.explanation: The singlet state exhibits perfect anti-correlation: when measured in the same basis, if Alice gets |0⟩, Bob always gets |1⟩, and vice versa. This is why Bob needs to flip his bits when generating the shared key.

questions.question: What is 'device-independent' security in the context of E91?
options: The protocol works with any quantum device
Security is guaranteed even if the measurement devices are untrusted or imperfect
The devices don't need calibration
The protocol doesn't require any physical devices
questions.explanation: Device-independent security means the protocol's security can be proven solely from the observed Bell inequality violation, without trusting how the measurement devices work internally. Even if the devices are malicious or imperfect, observing S > 2 guarantees security.

questions.question: Why does E91 use three measurement bases for each party instead of just two?
options: To generate more key bits
To enable both key generation (matching bases) and Bell test (non-matching bases)
To make the protocol more secure
Three bases are mathematically simpler
questions.explanation: Three bases per party provide: (1) One matching basis pair for key generation, and (2) Four non-matching combinations that are optimal for the CHSH Bell test. This dual purpose allows simultaneous key generation and security verification.

questions.question: What is the expected CHSH value S for a perfect singlet state with optimal measurement angles?
questions.explanation: For a maximally entangled state (like the singlet) with optimal measurement angles (0°, 45°, 90° for Alice; 45°, 90°, 135° for Bob), the CHSH value reaches its quantum maximum of 2√2 ≈ 2.83, known as the Tsirelson bound.
